title: Fix: Change date format string for Windows compatibility
body:
# Fix date format string for Windows compatibility

## Problem
When running `interpreter --os` on Windows, the program crashes with a ValueError due to an incompatible date format string (`%-d`).

## Solution
Changed the date format string from `%-d` to `%d` to ensure compatibility with Windows systems.

## Changes
- Modified `interpreter/computer_use/loop.py` to use Windows-compatible date format
- Replaced `%-d` with `%d` in `strftime()` call

## Testing
Tested on:
- Windows 11
- Python 3.11
- open-interpreter 0.4.1

The fix allows the program to start normally with the `--os` flag on Windows.

Fixes #1495

step_plan.summary: Replace POSIX-only '%-d' day format specifier with a Windows-compatible approach in interpreter/computer_use/loop.py (update both module-level SYSTEM_PROMPT occurrences) and harden sampling_loop to construct a platform-agnostic date string so the --os flag no longer crashes on Windows.

steps.operation: Replace POSIX-only '%-d' with Windows-safe '%d' in the first module-level SYSTEM_PROMPT assignment
steps.reason: The '%-d' day specifier is POSIX-only and causes datetime.strftime to raise ValueError on Windows. Replacing it with '%d' prevents the crash when the module loads on Windows.
steps.side_effects: Day values 1-9 will become zero-padded (e.g., '03' instead of '3'), which is a cosmetic change to the prompt; any tests or code that assert the exact SYSTEM_PROMPT string will need updating. No API or runtime behavior other than prompt text is changed. Downstream code that reads SYSTEM_PROMPT will get the updated string.

steps.operation: Replace POSIX-only '%-d' with Windows-safe '%d' in the second module-level SYSTEM_PROMPT assignment
steps.reason: There are multiple module-level assignments that include the problematic specifier; updating the second occurrence ensures no remaining '%-d' instances remain that could crash on Windows or cause inconsistent behavior.
steps.side_effects: Same cosmetic zero-padding effect for single-digit days; tests asserting the old string must be updated. If the file intentionally reassigns SYSTEM_PROMPT later, this ensures every literal occurrence is fixed, reducing risk of runtime crashes or confusion from leftover incompatible strings.

steps.operation: Make sampling_loop construct or sanitize the date string in a platform-agnostic way before composing/sending the system prompt
steps.reason: sampling_loop is the runtime site that composes and sends the system prompt; defensively building the date string from datetime components (e.g., date.day) or using separate strftime calls (for weekday/month/year) avoids relying on platform-specific strftime flags and guarantees Windows compatibility even if other prompt definitions exist or are modified.
steps.side_effects: This makes the runtime prompt construction robust across platforms and avoids regressions if other SYSTEM_PROMPT values are changed later. Tests that inspect the runtime prompt may need updates to match the new date format (for example, change from non-padded to padded day or vice versa depending on chosen implementation). No API changes; minor behavioral change only for the prompt text. If other modules rely on the prior exact composition of the prompt string, they may need adjustments.
